I use that time's up to you,
but if you'd like questions, I suggest you, um,
give yourself some time at the end for those questions.
You, you have my submission in front of you? Yes, we do.
So I'll go through it reasonably quickly. Brilliant.
With a few war stories so that, uh,
you get a bit of an idea where we're at. Yeah.
Yeah. Good morning.
Thanks for the opportunity for coming,
uh, for us to be here.
I'm Andy Warren from Bloom, New Zealand Limited,
and I have got my colleagues, Louisa Vandenberg,
who I've worked with for 25 years, been right
around the world on many a mission
and Chris Claridge, who is well known on
the horticultural industry.
We also represent the New Zealand Fruit
Tree Importers Group.
We are the convener of that, which is a group of corporates
who are the key importers in New Zealand.
There's only about seven importers in the actual whole
live plant industry.
So we've been actively campaigning for complete overhaul
of the light plant import regulations
and system for about 10 years.
So what we ask here today,
and the key questions are an independent review in order
to the proposed plant health environment capability
facility, and the associated plant import process to be
co governed by the actual users of the facilities,
which is us as importers.
Secondly, an examination of an alternative business model
for live plant importing and diagnostic testing.
And the third thing is the import processes designed,
monitored, and managed in collaboration
with actual importers as opposed to anybody else.
So who are we? We, I've been personally importing
and exporting live plants for over 40 years,
and during that time, I've witnessed the degradation
of NPI services more recently due to an erosion
of technical capability and capacity,
and a very high turnover of staff
demonstrating a significant loss of institutional knowledge.
And that's a pretty critical issue.
We've got support today from key drives, including 85%
of the, at least 85% of the Apple industry exports,
including New Zealand Fruit Tree Company,
the largest importer in New Zealand,
the Yummy Fruit Company, poor painter, market gardeners,
fresh Max Fruit Craft, that's the Bostock Group, Mr.
Apple Craigmore Investments, FreshCo Southern Produce,
New Zealand, China Fruit Company,
and the largest citrus operator in New Zealand
who do about 50% of export first fresh.
We've also the support of the actual facilitator
of NPIs import reform workshops,
professor Hamish Gar from Lincoln University.
Our concerns, we have 12 key points that we wish to present.
Even one of them would be enough to suggest
to MPI that we need to review
and look at this whole process.
First point is imports are currently largely frozen,
and, uh, we note that 85%
of new plant right variety rights are from
offshore breeding.
So the importance of in import cannot be underestimated.
It's simply unaffordable.
Two, less than 50% of the available MPIL three B
post entry quarantine space is currently being utilized.
And that's likely to go down as we use up the old, uh,
charging areas that may get down to as low as 30%
three NPIs plant quarantine facility charges
are at least three times
more than any other equivalent facility around the world.
Four over 1000 import health standards have
just been suspended.
MPI had not completed one new live plant import health
standard in the past 18 months, despite many requests,
we still don't have an import health standard for pears,
apple, and pears for over 26 years,
despite many requests.
And the root stock that goes with that.
This is a huge issue for our premier apple
and peer breeding organization.
Prevar up in Hawkes Bay. Paul will talk about that five.
The IHS review,
that's the import health standard review process,
needs review and radical change.
I think MPI actually agree with that.
Given a thousand standards have been suspended,
and it's taking MPI somewhere between 8,000
and 15,000 man hours
or woman hours to do one live plant
in import health standard, we've gotta look at a change.
And that's why in 0.6, we proposed the use of
AI using an ontological database, uh, arrangement.
This has already been done in Australia,
and that's why we're suggesting we need
to look at other countries to do that rather than trying
to develop it ourselves.
Seven, there's insufficient recognition
of offshore clean plant facilities.
There's about eight, which are the source of most of the new
varieties for new crops in New Zealand.
Ke fruit, apples, stone fruit, citrus, avocados, all
that kind of thing, and all the testing that's done
by them offshore.
So the point is to get it done before it even gets here.
For example, nack tobo in Netherlands has only three
of the 30 tests required for prunus recognized by MPI.
So there's consequently massive duplication
and a big cost to industry, something Mr.
Hoard was quite concerned about.
So number eight, there is no evidence
of growth in diagnostic testing demand and like,
and unlikely to be any increase in surveillance testing
unless more budget is put together for associated.
So, uh, surveillance programs,
we see none of that at this point.
Nine MPI have spent over the last five years,
$102 million on planning, designing
and developing a business case
for the PHCC project in Mount Albert
and projecting a total cost of between 500 million
and a billion dollars.
By the way, there's been four separate design
efforts on that already.
So we are, we are really concerned about the institutional
knowledge issue there.
We would just keep on going round and round 10.
The business case for PHCC was written without industry
collaboration and the projected cost is up
to a hundred times more than a privately designed testing
facility with cutting edge testing
and diagnostic capability.
11. There, there's been an exponential change,
and I mean, a totally rapid change in testing
and PE deduction methodologies in the last five
or six years since that 2019 start to that project.
So things have changed markedly.
We've been through COVID, um,
and this is eclipsed their approach to risk management
to the extent that NPIs testing charges now are in excess
of 15 times,
and I say 15 times what a private
facility would charge.
And we've put together a very, um,
complex appendix, um, which Mr.
Claridge can speak to.
We've provided a full cost of testing model by a third party
and a direct comparison using cherries as an example.
So in the case of cherries, MPI $20,000
and now new approach academically endorse
and biosecurity security, Safeway $1,500.
So you can see there's a massive change in costs
that we've come up with.
And 12 as recently of last month, NPR are proposing
to increase the cost of their services, 300% increase to
certification fees alone due to a historic deficit.
So therefore, to be blunt,
we have no confidence in NPIs ability
to restrain costs over time.
The questions that need answering
why has MPI ignored the 12 points we have raised.
They cannot be unaware of them.
The formal live plant import pathway
have actually proved incredibly safe.
We haven't had an in, uh, an incursion in 30 years.
And the big risks right now,
and you'll hear it in the paper all the time,
are from freight, e-commerce, passengers,
and dare I say, it's smuggling.
And we can prove
that we've notified NPI on numerous times about potential

And we can quote you that we know about it.
So why aren't these a priority?
Why have MPI failed to engage

When we went up there one time, they thought

not actually industry.
So there's a bit of an issue there.
So the recent import reform workshops are an example

The issues are already in well known with similar

So why we asked, do we not build a collaborative model
of testing with other providers?
So Andy, you're coming up to nine minutes now.
You want, you're coming up to nine minutes. Yep.
No, I'm of your I'm in time. Yeah.
So testing and diagnostics,
there have been already exponential
changes in methodologies.
Gene sequencing is no longer limited to viruses,
and we can do a lot of other things.
So we have no confidence in the outdated design of PHEC.
There are at least seven companies in China

So you're testing a virus, same as a human virus.
It's pretty cheap. So the volume of testing done

It's still, when we've done an analysis

that has not changed.

And this has largely been enabled by the explosion

There are other tools that can help detect

MPIs, the regulator, funder,

industry has no mechanism to hold NPI to account

look at ville that was three years behind

So, um, and we see it as irresponsible

it could probably only be described as a vanity project.
People tend to hold onto things.
The facility itself will not increase exports.
We propose a pause
and proceedings like the Cook State Fer project
to re-scope the project in consideration

with a revamp laboratory plan
and PEQ facility that, that uses established
offshore design and builds a system,
not like the bespoke system

which quite frankly is not too good to look at. I'm gonna,
I'm gonna stop you there and go to questions

how much engagement has there been with bi?
